name: Zimbabwe Cyber and Data Protection Act
description: Self-assessment against key data protection requirements for Zimbabwean organisations.

sections.name: Governance and accountability
sections.description: Leadership ownership, policies, roles, and oversight for data protection.

questions.text: Has your organisation assigned a person accountable for data protection?
questions.helpText: This can be a formal DPO role or a clearly named responsible officer.

guidance.title: Appoint and empower a data protection accountability owner
guidance.risk: Without a designated accountable owner, privacy decisions, incident escalation, and regulator engagement are inconsistent and delayed.
actions: Issue a signed appointment letter naming the data protection lead and clearly defining authority to approve privacy controls.
Publish a RACI matrix for legal, security, HR, and product teams that identifies who approves, executes, and reviews privacy decisions.
Add a standing privacy governance agenda item to monthly leadership meetings with owner-led status reporting on open risks.
Define succession and backup coverage so the accountability role is always staffed during leave or staff transitions.
evidence: Signed appointment letter and approved role description.
Current RACI matrix version with document control metadata.
Leadership meeting minutes showing privacy risk review and assigned actions.
Business continuity or succession plan naming alternate accountability owners.

questions.text: Do you maintain a documented data protection and privacy policy?
questions.helpText: The policy should be approved, current, and known by relevant teams.

guidance.title: Maintain an approved and operational privacy policy
guidance.risk: If the policy is outdated or not embedded into operations, teams handle personal data inconsistently and create avoidable compliance failures.
actions: Publish a version-controlled privacy policy that maps each control statement to a process owner and review frequency.
Set a mandatory annual policy review workflow with legal and security sign-off before the policy expiry date.
Link policy requirements to onboarding and operational procedures used by HR, product, and support teams.
Distribute policy updates through tracked communications and require acknowledgement from relevant employees.
evidence: Current approved policy version with owner, approver, and effective date.
Policy review records including legal/security comments and closure status.
Procedure documents cross-referencing policy clauses.
Communication logs and employee acknowledgement reports.

questions.text: Does leadership review data protection performance on a regular cadence?
questions.helpText: Examples include quarterly risk committee or board updates.

guidance.title: Operationalize leadership oversight for privacy performance
guidance.risk: Absent leadership review allows unresolved control failures to persist and weakens defensibility during audits or investigations.
actions: Define quarterly privacy KPI/KRI reporting with thresholds for training completion, DSR timeliness, incidents, and overdue remediation.
Escalate high-risk privacy items to the executive risk register with due dates and accountable owners.
Track governance decisions and remediation commitments in a central action log reviewed at each meeting.
Require leadership sign-off for risk acceptance decisions where controls cannot be implemented immediately.
evidence: Quarterly dashboards showing privacy KPIs/KRIs and threshold breaches.
Risk register entries with owner, treatment plan, and target completion dates.
Meeting minutes with decisions, approvals, and action follow-up.
Documented risk acceptance memos signed by designated approvers.

sections.name: Lawful processing and consent
sections.description: Legal basis, consent controls, and purpose limitation practices.

questions.text: Have you documented lawful bases for key personal data processing activities?
questions.helpText: A processing register can map each activity to its lawful basis.

guidance.title: Document lawful basis for each processing activity
guidance.risk: Processing without clear lawful basis mapping creates legal exposure and weakens response capability for complaints.
actions: Build a processing activity register that lists purpose, categories of data, lawful basis, and responsible business owner.
Introduce a privacy review gate in project intake so new processing cannot go live without lawful basis validation.
Perform semi-annual sampling of active systems to confirm processing activities match the registered lawful basis.
Update privacy notices and internal records immediately when lawful basis changes due to product or legal changes.
evidence: Current processing activity register export with last review timestamp.
Project intake records showing lawful basis approval before deployment.
Sampling/audit reports with findings and corrective actions.
Change history of privacy notices and lawful basis updates.

questions.text: Do consent collection channels record when and how consent was obtained?
questions.helpText: Include digital forms, call centres, and offline channels where relevant.

guidance.title: Capture auditable consent records across all channels
guidance.risk: Inability to prove consent validity undermines legal defensibility and may require processing suspension.
actions: Configure digital consent capture to store timestamp, policy version, source channel, and purpose at the individual record level.
Implement call-centre scripting and CRM fields that require explicit consent capture before proceeding with optional processing.
Create a withdrawal workflow that propagates consent changes to downstream systems within a defined SLA.
Run monthly reconciliation between consent records and active marketing or profiling lists to remove unauthorized records.
evidence: Database schema or application screenshots showing consent metadata fields.
Call-centre SOP and call logs with recorded consent statements.
Workflow records demonstrating withdrawal processing and completion times.
Monthly reconciliation reports with exceptions and resolution evidence.

questions.text: Do teams check that personal data is only used for stated purposes?
questions.helpText: This should include controls before re-using data for new initiatives.

guidance.title: Enforce purpose limitation in change and analytics workflows
guidance.risk: Repurposing personal data without assessment can violate legal obligations and trigger customer trust issues.
actions: Add a mandatory purpose-compatibility assessment step to change management tickets for new data uses.
Block analytics dataset access unless the approved purpose tag matches the requesting use case.
Require legal/privacy approval for secondary use scenarios such as model training or cross-sell targeting.
Perform quarterly access/use reviews to detect and remediate unauthorized purpose expansion.
evidence: Change tickets with completed purpose-compatibility assessments.
Access-control rules and logs showing purpose-based enforcement.
Approval records for secondary use exceptions.
Quarterly review reports and remediation closure records.

sections.name: Data Subject Rights
sections.description: Processes and controls for handling access, correction, deletion, and objection requests.

questions.text: Do you maintain a controlled workflow to intake, validate, and track data subject rights requests?
questions.helpText: Cover access, correction, deletion, objection, and restriction request types.

guidance.title: Implement an end-to-end DSR case management workflow
guidance.risk: Unstructured DSR handling causes missed deadlines, inconsistent outcomes, and inability to evidence compliance.
actions: Deploy a centralized case workflow with request type, intake date, due date, owner, and status fields.
Define identity verification steps for each request channel and require completion before disclosure or deletion actions.
Set automated SLA reminders and escalation rules for cases approaching legal deadlines.
Maintain templated responses for approvals, partial disclosures, and lawful refusals with legal review checkpoints.
evidence: DSR tracker export showing case lifecycle fields and timestamps.
Identity verification SOPs and sample completed verification records.
SLA breach dashboard or escalation logs.
Approved response templates and legal sign-off records.

questions.text: Can you fulfil DSR requests completely across all systems containing personal data?
questions.helpText: This includes production systems, backups, and processor-held data where applicable.

guidance.title: Assure complete and timely DSR fulfilment across data estates
guidance.risk: Partial fulfilment leaves residual personal data in scope systems, increasing complaint and enforcement risk.
actions: Map DSR fulfilment playbooks to each in-scope system, including who executes extraction, correction, deletion, or restriction.
Integrate processor notification steps so outsourced systems are included in fulfilment timelines.
Run quarterly fulfilment simulations to test end-to-end retrieval and deletion coverage.
Implement quality checks requiring peer review before closing high-impact DSR cases.
evidence: System-specific DSR playbooks with owner assignments.
Processor communication logs for DSR propagation.
Simulation reports with gap remediation actions.
Closed case records with peer-review and closure approval fields.

sections.name: Data Inventory & Classification
sections.description: Discovery, cataloguing, and classification of personal data assets and flows.

questions.text: Do you maintain an up-to-date data inventory of systems, datasets, and data flows containing personal data?
questions.helpText: Inventory should include source, purpose, location, and transfer points.

guidance.title: Maintain a complete and current personal data inventory
guidance.risk: Unknown data stores prevent effective risk management, DSR fulfilment, and breach impact analysis.
actions: Create a centralized inventory that records system owner, data categories, storage location, and inbound/outbound transfers.
Require inventory updates as a release criterion for new systems or schema changes involving personal data.
Run periodic discovery scans and reconcile results against the inventory for missing assets.
Track data flow diagrams for high-risk processes and review them after major architecture changes.
evidence: Current inventory export with change history and ownership metadata.
Release checklist records proving inventory update completion.
Discovery scan reports and reconciliation tickets.
Approved data flow diagrams with last review dates.

questions.text: Is personal data classified using a defined sensitivity taxonomy that drives control requirements?
questions.helpText: Classification should trigger access, encryption, and retention rules.

guidance.title: Apply enforceable data classification for personal data
guidance.risk: Without consistent classification, sensitive data may receive inadequate protection or excessive access.
actions: Define classification tiers with explicit handling requirements for access controls, encryption, and sharing.
Tag data assets with classification labels in databases, storage platforms, and BI tools.
Enforce policy-as-code checks that block deployments when required classification tags are missing.
Train data owners on classification decision criteria and exception approval workflow.
evidence: Published classification standard and handling matrix.
System screenshots or exports showing classification tags.
CI/CD or policy engine logs demonstrating enforcement failures and approvals.
Training attendance and assessment records for data owners.

sections.name: Security Safeguards
sections.description: Technical and organizational measures protecting personal data confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

questions.text: Are access controls for personal data based on least privilege and regularly reviewed?
questions.helpText: Include privileged access, shared accounts, and joiner-mover-leaver processes.

guidance.title: Harden access controls for personal data systems
guidance.risk: Excessive or stale access increases the chance of unauthorized disclosure and insider misuse.
actions: Implement role-based access control with segregation of duties for administrative and data export functions.
Enforce MFA for privileged and remote access to systems processing personal data.
Run monthly access recertification for high-risk systems and disable unapproved or dormant accounts.
Eliminate shared accounts or place them behind managed vault workflows with session logging.
evidence: RBAC matrix and system permission mappings.
MFA policy and authentication logs demonstrating enforcement.
Access recertification reports and deprovisioning tickets.
Privileged access vault logs and account ownership attestations.

questions.text: Do you protect personal data with appropriate encryption, endpoint controls, and secure configuration baselines?
questions.helpText: Cover data at rest, data in transit, and endpoint data leakage controls.

guidance.title: Implement layered technical safeguards for personal data
guidance.risk: Weak encryption or insecure configurations materially increase breach likelihood and impact.
actions: Encrypt personal data at rest using approved algorithms and managed key rotation schedules.
Enforce TLS for internal and external data transmission paths handling personal data.
Deploy endpoint DLP and removable media controls for teams handling high-sensitivity records.
Baseline hardening standards for servers and databases, with automated drift detection and remediation.
evidence: Encryption standards and key management rotation logs.
Network/security configuration exports proving TLS enforcement.
Endpoint DLP policy configurations and alert investigation records.
Hardening benchmark reports and drift remediation tickets.

sections.name: Incident & Breach Management
sections.description: Preparedness, detection, response, notification, and lessons learned for personal data incidents.

questions.text: Do you operate a tested incident response playbook for personal data incidents?
questions.helpText: The process should define triage, escalation, legal review, and communication steps.

guidance.title: Operationalize and test data incident response procedures
guidance.risk: An untested playbook causes delayed containment and inconsistent decision-making during high-pressure incidents.
actions: Document a privacy incident playbook with severity levels, decision authorities, and communication channels.
Integrate legal, security, and communications teams into a single on-call escalation workflow.
Conduct at least two tabletop exercises annually covering ransomware and unauthorized disclosure scenarios.
Track corrective actions from exercises and incidents to verified closure within agreed timelines.
evidence: Approved incident response playbook and contact roster.
On-call schedule and escalation runbook records.
Tabletop exercise agendas, participant lists, and outcome reports.
Post-incident action register with closure evidence.

questions.text: Can your team assess reportability and notify regulators/data subjects within required timelines?
questions.helpText: Include criteria for harm assessment and notification approval workflow.

guidance.title: Deliver compliant breach assessment and notification
guidance.risk: Late or incomplete breach notifications can trigger enforcement action and reputational harm.
actions: Define a breach reportability decision tree with legal thresholds and mandatory consultation points.
Use notification templates pre-approved by legal and communications for regulators and affected individuals.
Implement a timer-based workflow from incident detection to notification submission with documented checkpoints.
Run periodic quality reviews of past incidents to verify timeline adherence and notification completeness.
evidence: Reportability decision tree and legal approval records.
Notification templates and version history.
Incident timeline logs with decision timestamps.
Quality review reports and remediation actions from prior incidents.

sections.name: Third-Party & Data Sharing
sections.description: Due diligence, contractual controls, and monitoring of processors and data-sharing partners.

questions.text: Do you perform privacy and security due diligence before onboarding third parties that process personal data?
questions.helpText: Apply risk-based review depth depending on data sensitivity and processing scale.

guidance.title: Institutionalize third-party privacy due diligence
guidance.risk: Onboarding processors without due diligence transfers unmanaged privacy risk into your processing chain.
actions: Adopt a risk-tiering questionnaire covering data types, sub-processors, breach history, and control maturity.
Require review of independent assurance artifacts such as SOC reports or equivalent control attestations.
Block procurement approval until privacy/security findings have an agreed remediation plan and owner.
Reassess high-risk vendors annually and after major service or ownership changes.
evidence: Completed due diligence assessments with risk ratings.
Assurance reports and control-gap analysis documents.
Procurement workflow records showing privacy/security sign-off.
Annual reassessment reports and updated risk treatment plans.

questions.text: Are data sharing and processor agreements complete, current, and enforceable?
questions.helpText: Contracts should include purpose limits, breach notification, security controls, and audit rights.

guidance.title: Strengthen contractual controls for external data processing
guidance.risk: Weak or outdated agreements limit legal recourse and hinder coordinated response when incidents occur.
actions: Standardize contract clauses for purpose limitation, confidentiality, security controls, sub-processor approval, and audit rights.
Track agreement expiry dates and renewal milestones in a contract register with automated reminders.
Validate that executed contracts align with actual data flows and current service architecture.
Enforce incident notification clauses with tested communication channels and response obligations.
evidence: Executed contracts and approved clause library.
Contract register with renewal and owner metadata.
Data flow-to-contract mapping review records.
Vendor incident communication test results or drill reports.

sections.name: Data Retention & Disposal
sections.description: Retention schedules, deletion workflows, and secure disposal controls for personal data.

questions.text: Do you enforce documented retention periods for each personal data category?
questions.helpText: Retention should align to legal obligations and business need with clear ownership.

guidance.title: Define and enforce retention schedules
guidance.risk: Over-retention increases breach impact and regulatory risk, while under-retention can break legal obligations.
actions: Create a retention schedule that maps each data category to legal basis, retention duration, and owner.
Implement system-level retention rules and archival controls that automatically enforce scheduled expiry.
Require legal approval for retention exceptions and set explicit sunset dates for each exception.
Review retention rule effectiveness quarterly against actual data age distributions.
evidence: Approved retention schedule and governance approval records.
System configuration exports showing retention enforcement settings.
Exception register with legal approvals and expiry dates.
Quarterly metrics showing aged data reduction and exception closure.

questions.text: Do you securely dispose of personal data across live systems, archives, and media?
questions.helpText: Include logical deletion, cryptographic erasure, and physical media destruction where relevant.

guidance.title: Execute verifiable and secure data disposal
guidance.risk: Incomplete disposal leaves recoverable personal data that can be exposed through compromise or mishandling.
actions: Implement deletion workflows that cover production, backups, archives, and replicated environments.
Use cryptographic erasure or approved sanitization procedures for storage media leaving service.
Require dual-control approval for bulk deletion jobs involving high-volume personal data.
Sample-test disposed datasets to confirm irrecoverability and document test outcomes.
evidence: Deletion workflow logs spanning all data environments.
Media sanitization or destruction certificates from approved providers.
Approval records for bulk deletion operations.
Validation test reports confirming disposal effectiveness.

sections.name: Training & Awareness
sections.description: Role-based privacy training, awareness reinforcement, and behavioral control validation.

questions.text: Do employees and contractors receive role-based privacy and data handling training?
questions.helpText: Training should differ for high-impact roles such as HR, support, engineering, and marketing.

guidance.title: Deliver role-specific privacy capability training
guidance.risk: Generic or infrequent training leaves high-risk teams without practical knowledge to prevent handling errors.
actions: Define training curricula by role with required modules on lawful use, incident escalation, and secure handling.
Require completion of role-based training within onboarding windows and annual refresh cycles.
Apply minimum pass-score assessments and remedial training for failed attempts.
Report completion and assessment metrics to department heads for accountability.
evidence: Role-to-curriculum mapping document and approved learning objectives.
LMS completion reports with timestamps and user populations.
Assessment score reports and remedial completion records.
Management review records of training compliance metrics.

questions.text: Do you measure whether privacy awareness activities improve employee behavior?
questions.helpText: Use measurable outcomes beyond attendance, such as incident trends and campaign results.

guidance.title: Measure and improve privacy awareness effectiveness
guidance.risk: Awareness programs without outcome measurement do not reliably reduce privacy handling errors.
actions: Define behavioral metrics such as misdirected disclosures, policy violations, and incident reporting lag.
Run targeted awareness campaigns for recurring failure patterns identified in incidents or audits.
Use post-campaign quizzes or simulations to validate retention of key handling practices.
Adjust training content quarterly based on measured behavior and control failures.
evidence: Awareness KPI definitions and periodic measurement reports.
Campaign materials with targeted audience and schedule.
Quiz/simulation results and trend analysis.
Content update logs linked to observed behavioral outcomes.

sections.name: Monitoring & Continuous Improvement
sections.description: Control monitoring, internal assurance, and remediation lifecycle management.

questions.text: Do you monitor privacy controls with defined metrics and trigger remediation when thresholds are breached?
questions.helpText: Metrics should cover preventive, detective, and corrective controls.

guidance.title: Establish measurable privacy control monitoring
guidance.risk: Without control monitoring, issues remain undetected and mature into repeat audit findings or incidents.
actions: Define control KPIs with thresholds for DSR SLA, retention exceptions, access recertification, and incident response timeliness.
Automate monthly control metric collection from ticketing, IAM, SIEM, and case management tools.
Trigger remediation tickets automatically for threshold breaches with assigned owners and due dates.
Review control performance trends in governance forums and adjust controls where repeated breaches occur.
evidence: KPI catalogue with thresholds and control ownership.
Automated metric extracts or dashboard snapshots.
Remediation tickets linked to threshold breach events.
Governance meeting records showing trend review decisions.

questions.text: Do you run periodic internal assessments and close remediation actions to verified completion?
questions.helpText: Assessments may include internal audit, control self-assessment, and targeted deep dives.

guidance.title: Run recurring assurance and validate remediation closure
guidance.risk: Findings that are tracked but not validated to closure create recurring non-conformance and audit fatigue.
actions: Schedule annual privacy control self-assessments and risk-based internal audits across critical processing areas.
Track all findings in a remediation register with root cause, owner, due date, and validation method.
Require independent validation before marking corrective actions complete.
Analyze recurring findings quarterly to identify systemic process redesign opportunities.
evidence: Approved assurance plan and completed assessment reports.
Central remediation register with current status and aging.
Validation records signed by independent reviewers.
Quarterly recurring-findings analysis with action plans.
